method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Tromelin Island is known in French as Île Tromelin and was formerly called the Isle of Sand.[1] Its present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette Dauphine.[1] Tromelin reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of about 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, located roughly 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It is sparsely vegetated, described as having a few low bushes and no trees, and has also been characterized as a nearly “desert island”.[5] The island is uninhabited apart from a small scientific and meteorological station, which has operated since 1949 and supports cyclone monitoring and regional weather forecasting.[4] It is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands.[4]  

The island’s surface and topography are influenced by coral-island sediment processes, including aeolian sand transport, with strong winds on Tromelin concentrated between directions of about 100° and 140°.[3] Its ecological significance includes nesting seabirds and sea turtles, and it has been identified as a site for conservation and environmental research.[1][4][5]  

Tromelin’s native ecosystem was severely affected by introduced rats, which reduced native vegetation and contributed to the disappearance of six of the island’s eight native bird species.[1] Before eradication, only a few hundred pairs of masked and red-footed boobies remained among the island’s seabirds.[2] French authorities removed the rodents in 2005 using poisoned bait, completing the eradication in about a month.[1][2] Since the removal of rats, native plants have begun to recover and three bird species have returned without additional seabird-attraction measures such as decoys or recorded calls.[1][2]

==Fauna and flora==
The island’s native terrestrial fauna is dominated by seabirds, while nesting green sea turtles (''Chelonia mydas'') are the principal non-avian vertebrates recorded there; no native terrestrial mammals, reptiles or amphibians are known.[1] Before the establishment of invasive rats, Tromelin supported about six to eight breeding seabird species, but by 2005 only two booby species were still breeding, and with limited success.[1] After rat eradication, the breeding seabird community recovered to seven species by 2022, while the number of breeding pairs rose from 353 to 4,758.[1] The masked booby (''Sula dactylatra melanops''), an endemic subspecies, is among the island’s important breeding seabirds.[1] Surveys from 2017 to 2021 also recorded eleven non-breeding seabird species, including roseate tern, common tern, Saunders's tern, lesser crested tern, brown skua, Mascarene petrel, great and lesser frigatebirds, and both white-tailed and red-tailed tropicbirds.[3] Great and lesser frigatebirds use the island as regular roosting sites, while the two tropicbird species have occasionally landed or displayed, suggesting possible future breeding settlement.[3] Mice are the only introduced mammals reported as still present on the island after rat removal.[3]

The vegetation is a simple littoral community adapted to wind, salt spray and limited freshwater, with naturally low species diversity.[1] The native flora historically consisted mainly of the shrub ''Tournefortia argentea''—also known as ''Heliotropium foertherianum'' or tree heliotrope—together with fewer than ten herbaceous species and no native trees.[1] Rat predation and browsing had reduced native plant cover substantially before eradication, but monitoring later recorded recovery to seven native taxa by 2013.[1] The rebound included increases in the density and height of ''Tournefortia argentea'', improving habitat structure for wildlife.[1] A few introduced coconut palms (''Cocos nucifera'') remain, although they do not dominate the vegetation.[1] Conservation measures focus on preventing renewed introductions of invasive species, because the recovery of seabirds and vegetation is closely linked to biosecurity on the island.[1][4]

==History==
The island was recorded by France in the 1720s, when the navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée named it Île de Sable, or “Isle of Sand”.[1]

Its best-known historical episode began in July 1761, when the French ship Utile was wrecked there while carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people.[1][2] About 20 sailors and at least 70 enslaved people died in the wreck; the enslaved people who died were unable to escape flooding below deck because the hatches had been locked or nailed down.[2] A group of survivors remained stranded on the island for 15 years, until eight people—seven formerly enslaved women and a boy born during the ordeal—were rescued in 1776.[2][3] The rescue was carried out by Captain Chevalier de Tromelin, who took the survivors to Mauritius, where they were later freed.[3] The island was subsequently named after Tromelin in his honour.[3]

Later archaeological investigations examined both the wreck and the castaways’ settlement, including a French expedition of archaeologists and divers sponsored by UNESCO as part of a commemoration of the struggle against slavery.[2] Investigators identified walls of dwellings built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds and shellfish.[2] Historical accounts cited in connection with the site also recorded British sailors observing the remains of “stone” houses and carefully arranged graves.[2] The episode has since been treated in documentaries, archaeological reports and historical commentary as a symbol of slavery, abandonment and survival.[5]

France dates its claim to sovereignty over Tromelin to 29 November 1776, when the ship Dauphine arrived at the island.[1] Mauritius bases its claim on the argument that Tromelin should have been ceded to the United Kingdom under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 and should not have continued to be administered by France as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1] France later reinforced its presence on the island with meteorological facilities and a landing strip during the mid-20th century.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate (Köppen Aw), with warm conditions year-round and a marked wet season from November to April associated with the intertropical convergence zone.[2]  
The mean annual temperature is about 26.7 °C, with only limited seasonal variation because of the island's tropical oceanic setting.[2]  
Annual rainfall is approximately 1,074 mm, falling mainly during the austral summer; February is among the wettest months, while May to October is comparatively dry.[2]  
Prevailing south-easterly trade winds are common, often exceeding 8 m/s and contributing to persistent evaporation and exposed conditions on the low, flat coral island.[2]  
The island's position in the western Indian Ocean makes it useful for monitoring tropical weather systems, particularly cyclones affecting Madagascar and nearby islands.[2][4]  
A staffed meteorological station has operated on Tromelin for regional weather observation and cyclone forecasting, although earlier infrastructure was damaged by a cyclone and later rebuilt.[2][4]
